briefing.opening: Good morning. As a climate change expert spokesperson and science journalist, I will begin today's climate briefing on July 14, 2026. Today, we will focus on the intensifying extreme weather events worldwide and their widespread impacts, as well as the diverse domestic and international efforts to address them, alongside the challenges we face.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensification of Global Extreme Weather Events and Human Casualties
sections.content: Recently, on the Korean Peninsula, a capricious monsoon rain was observed immediately after a heatwave reaching 40 degrees Celsius [2], and torrential downpours of up to 120mm fell nationwide due to the influence of Typhoon Bavi (No. 9) [126]. In Europe and the United States, severe situations continue, including human casualties and radio service disruptions due to 'killer heatwaves' [23]. Notably, in Europe, a 'silent disaster' occurred where approximately 10,000 people died from heatwaves in just one month [12]. In France, record-breaking heatwaves led to a shortage of cooling water for nuclear power plants, resulting in the unprecedented reduction of reactor operations [6]. In the UK, a record heatwave exceeding 37 degrees Celsius in June led to a shocking outcome of 1,140 deaths [135, 147]. These extreme weather events are analyzed to be caused by climate change [128, 133], and suspicions of concealing power grid outage risks have also been raised [148], clearly demonstrating the reality of the climate crisis.

sections.title: ⚠️ Widespread Economic and Ecological Threats Posed by the Climate Crisis
sections.content: Climate change is not merely an anomaly in weather; it affects all aspects of our lives. Domestically, horticultural crop cultivation areas are moving northward, and even high-altitude farming is experiencing disruptions due to heatwaves [1, 15, 28]. The fishing industry is also directly impacted, with the death of Manila clams in Taean's waters due to a 56-day heatwave [16]. Internationally, alongside warnings that rising temperatures could increase coffee prices [11], the possibility of global food prices skyrocketing by 2028 has been raised [3]. Economic ripple effects such as threats to crop nutrition, increases in urban water utility fees, and a 6% reduction in Italian production are also predicted [132, 138, 141]. Ecosystems also face severe threats: the Pacific gray whale population has collapsed by half, and a 'catastrophic die-off' due to disappearing food sources has been warned [19]. In Australia, shark bite incidents are increasing, and in Alaska, invasive predators are destroying iconic fish species [130, 131]. The cascading threats posed by the climate crisis are expanding into a global catastrophe.

sections.title: 🌱 Diverse Domestic and International Efforts and Citizen Participation for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, domestic and international efforts towards a carbon-neutral society continue. Domestically, the 2026 Local Conference of Youth on Climate Change (LCOY KOREA) successfully concluded, directing the voices of future generations towards COP31 [5]. Jeju City emphasized that citizen action is the greatest force in achieving its 2035 carbon neutrality goal [26]. Bucheon City is expanding citizen participation by establishing a carbon neutrality response platform [32, 39, 44, 45, 77], and in Gwangmyeong City, citizen-led carbon neutrality practices are actively taking place, with participants in the 'Climate Militia' exceeding 20,000 [33, 34, 35, 66, 78]. Taean County is piloting the cultivation of alternative clam species resilient to climate change [7, 9, 14, 27], and Gyeonggi Province is developing 'K-Chrysanthemums' that are resilient to heatwaves [18], demonstrating concrete efforts for climate change adaptation. Furthermore, international solidarity and cooperation are active, with Korea Water Resources Corporation strengthening climate crisis response and water cooperation with the Global Green Growth Institute [24], and the KCL consortium assisting in strengthening the carbon neutrality capabilities of Thailand's electrical and electronics industry [41, 47].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and the Dilemma of Energy Policy
sections.content: The government is accelerating the expansion of renewable energy, announcing its goal to achieve 100GW of renewable energy by 2030 through its second-half economic growth strategy [86, 104, 119]. Large-scale solar power generation projects are being promoted at Lake Sihwa and Lake Pyeongtaek [107], and Korea East-West Power is spurring the expansion of renewable energy infrastructure by constructing the nation's first 60MWh 'Renewable Energy Additional Connection ESS' [98, 113, 122]. However, despite these efforts, there are concerns that a 'red light' has been lit on achieving the 2035 carbon reduction target, attributed to the insufficient pace of renewable energy deployment [123]. The dilemma regarding the direction of energy policy is growing, particularly between civil society, which opposes the construction of Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) at the decommissioned Samcheonpo Thermal Power Plant site and urges a transition to renewable energy [95, 116], and the government's move to re-promote new nuclear power plant construction based on the judgment that renewable energy alone cannot meet electricity demand [100, 109].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Change Awareness and Response Challenges
sections.content: In the international community, alongside positive signals of renewable energy transition, cases demonstrating a gap in climate crisis awareness have also been reported. The growth of renewable energy is prominent, with solar power generation surpassing nuclear power for the first time to take the top spot in the European Union (EU) electricity market [112]. Global corporations like Taiwan's TSMC and Google are also participating in carbon emission reduction through renewable energy procurement and solar power utilization [117, 120, 124]. However, an analysis by The Guardian [127, 147] that most UK media outlets omitted mentioning the climate crisis in their reports on the record heatwave in June raises important questions about the role and responsibility of the media in raising public awareness of the climate crisis. Research indicating that the experience of climate change better captures dangerous shifts than mere numbers [129] suggests the need for new approaches to climate crisis communication. Furthermore, the global pressure that climate change exerts on critical infrastructure and financial systems [136, 145] demonstrates the urgent need for international cooperation and the development of practical countermeasures.

briefing.closing: As you have seen in today's briefing, we are facing an unprecedented climate crisis, but at the same time, various efforts and innovations are underway to overcome it. We will continue to confront the reality of climate change, seek effective solutions based on scientific data, and do our best to encourage everyone's participation. This concludes today's climate briefing.
